description: Timestamp precision is part of the JCS-canonicalized byte string. Two bodies whose timestamps represent the same logical instant but differ in fractional precision produce different canonical strings and therefore different content_hash values. The producer MUST truncate every timestamp to canonical millisecond form (RFC-ACDP-0001 §5.3) before computing content_hash. This vector locks in both the nanosecond-string hash AND the millisecond-truncated hash so that implementations can verify they would produce the millisecond hash on a normal producer path AND can demonstrate the nanosecond divergence as a negative case. Scope: this fixture covers the PRODUCER-side truncation requirement only — the same millisecond-precision rule applies to the registry-assigned `created_at` field per RFC-ACDP-0001 §5.3 and is exercised by `can-007-registry-created-at.json`.

vectors.name: Nanosecond-precision expires_at — DIVERGENT (illustrative; producers MUST NOT emit this form)
vectors.description: If a producer fails to truncate timestamps before signing, the resulting content_hash binds to the exact serialized string. Reproducible implementations of `chrono`, `datetime`, etc. with default high-precision output land here.
vectors.rationale: If your library's API accepts a `chrono::DateTime<Utc>::now()` (nanosecond precision) and passes it through to JCS without truncation, this is the hash you produce. No other conformant implementation can reproduce it from the logical instant alone.

vectors.name: Millisecond-truncated expires_at — CANONICAL (the form producers MUST emit)
vectors.description: Same logical instant, truncated to canonical millisecond form per RFC-ACDP-0001 §5.3. This is the byte string a conformant producer commits to.
vectors.rationale: Library APIs MUST automatically apply this truncation. Producers SHOULD never see the nanosecond form on the publish path.
